section.title: The Temple Sermon
section.sectionRationale: Standing at the temple gate itself, Jeremiah confronts the nation's most cherished false security: the belief that God's presence in the building guarantees safety regardless of how they live. This sermon, delivered at the very seat of Judah's religious confidence, names the temple a den of robbers and warns that it can be destroyed exactly as Shiloh was, a warning Jesus Himself will later echo word for word.
section.thesis: Judah trusts in the temple's presence as a magical guarantee of safety while oppressing the vulnerable and worshipping other gods, but the LORD warns that a house that bears His Name is no substitute for obedience, and will be destroyed like Shiloh before it if the people do not truly amend their ways.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the call to amend ways and deeds rather than trust deceptive words; the warning of Shiloh's fate as precedent; the exposure of household idolatry to the Queen of Heaven; the declaration that obedience, not sacrifice, was always the true command; and the vision of Topheth turned into the Valley of Slaughter.

units.label: Amend your ways
units.note: do not trust in deceptive words: this is the temple of the LORD

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD, saying,
“Stand in the gate of the house of the LORD and proclaim this message: Hear the word of the LORD, all you people of Judah who enter through these gates to worship the LORD.
Thus says the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel: Correct your ways and deeds, and I will let you live in this place.

Do not trust in deceptive words, chanting: 
‘This is the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, 
the temple of the LORD.’
For if you really correct your ways and deeds, if you act justly toward one another,
if you no longer oppress the foreigner and the fatherless and the widow, and if you no longer shed innocent blood in this place or follow other gods to your own harm,
then I will let you live in this place, in the land that I gave to your fathers forever and ever.
But look, you keep trusting in deceptive words to no avail.
Will you steal and murder, commit adultery and perjury, burn incense to Baal, and follow other gods that you have not known,
and then come and stand before Me in this house, which bears My Name, and say, ‘We are delivered, so we can continue with all these abominations’?
Has this house, which bears My Name, become a {{beit_peritsim|den of robbers}} in your sight? Yes, I too have seen it, declares the LORD.

units.label: Remember Shiloh
units.note: what I did there, I will do here

verses.text: But go now to the place in Shiloh where I first made a dwelling for My Name, and see what I did to it because of the wickedness of My people Israel.
And now, because you have done all these things, declares the LORD, and because I have spoken to you again and again but you would not listen, and I have called to you but you would not answer,
therefore what I did to Shiloh I will now do to the house that bears My Name, the house in which you trust, the place that I gave to you and your fathers.
And I will cast you out of My presence, just as I have cast out all your brothers, all the descendants of Ephraim.

units.label: The Queen of Heaven
units.note: do not pray for this people

verses.text: As for you, do not pray for these people, do not offer a plea or petition on their behalf, and do not beg Me, for I will not listen to you.
Do you not see what they are doing in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem?
The sons gather wood, the fathers light the fire, and the women knead the dough to make cakes for the Queen of Heaven; they pour out drink offerings to other gods to provoke Me to anger.
But am I the One they are provoking? declares the LORD. Is it not themselves they spite, to their own shame?

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, My anger and My fury will be poured out on this place, on man and beast, on the trees of the field and the produce of the land, and it will burn and not be extinguished.

units.label: Obedience, not sacrifice
units.note: the command was always to listen

verses.text: This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Add your burnt offerings to your other sacrifices and eat the meat yourselves!
For when I brought your fathers out of the land of Egypt, I did not merely command them about burnt offerings and sacrifices,
but this is what I commanded them: {{shama|Obey}} Me, and I will be your God, and you will be My people. You must walk in all the ways I have commanded you, so that it may go well with you.
Yet they did not listen or incline their ear, but they followed the stubborn inclinations of their own evil hearts. They went backward and not forward.
From the day your fathers came out of the land of Egypt until this day, I have sent you all My servants the prophets again and again.
Yet they would not listen to Me or incline their ear, but they stiffened their necks and did more evil than their fathers.
When you tell them all these things, they will not listen to you. When you call to them, they will not answer.
Therefore you must say to them, ‘This is the nation that would not listen to the voice of the LORD their God and would not receive correction. Truth has perished; it has disappeared from their lips.’

units.label: Topheth: the Valley of Slaughter
units.note: children in the fire, an evil never commanded

verses.text: Cut off your hair and throw it away. Raise up a lamentation on the barren heights, for the LORD has rejected and forsaken the generation of His wrath.’
For the people of Judah have done evil in My sight, declares the LORD. They have set up their abominations in the house that bears My Name, and so have defiled it.
They have built the high places of {{gehinnom|Topheth}} in the Valley of Hinnom so they could burn their sons and daughters in the fire, something I never commanded, nor did it even enter My mind.

So behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when this place will no longer be called Topheth and the Valley of Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter. For they will bury the dead in Topheth until there is no more room.
The corpses of this people will become food for the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth, and there will be no one to scare them away.
I will remove from the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem the sounds of joy and gladness and the voices of the bride and bridegroom, for the land will become a wasteland.”

“At that time,” declares the LORD, “the bones of the kings of Judah, the bones of the officials, the bones of the priests, the bones of the prophets, and the bones of the people of Jerusalem will be removed from their graves.

They will be exposed to the sun and moon, and to all the host of heaven which they have loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshiped. Their bones will not be gathered up or buried, but will become like dung lying on the ground.
And wherever I have banished them, the remnant of this evil family will choose death over life,” declares the LORD of Hosts.

beit_peritsim.gloss: Den of robbers, literally house of violent men. The charge is not that crimes happened in the temple itself but that the temple had become a hideout, a place people ran to for false security after committing crimes elsewhere, exactly the phrase Jesus quotes when He cleanses the temple, Mark 11:17.

shama.gloss: To hear, listen, and by extension obey; in Hebrew there is no separate word for obedience apart from truly hearing. God's original command at the exodus was fundamentally this, listen to My voice, making ritual sacrifice always secondary to a heart that actually hears and responds.

gehinnom.gloss: The Valley of Hinnom, site of the horrific child sacrifices to Molech at Topheth. This valley's name became, by the New Testament era, the Greek Gehenna, Jesus' most common word for final judgment and hell, a direct line from this literal valley of atrocity to the ultimate reality it came to symbolize.

basic.context: Jeremiah stands at the temple gate itself and confronts the crowd's favorite chant, this is the temple of the LORD, as a dangerous lie. God's presence in the building was never a magic guarantee of safety; it always required lives that matched the worship, justice for the vulnerable and loyalty to God alone. Jeremiah warns that the very temple they trust can be destroyed exactly as Shiloh, the earlier dwelling place of God's Name, already was.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real sermon delivered at a real location, the temple gate, confronting Judah's specific presumption that the temple's presence guaranteed unconditional protection. The hardest truth to hear plainly is that religious buildings, rituals, and even correct theological language, this is the temple of the LORD, provide no automatic safety when the life behind them is corrupt; God's own house can become, in His own words, a den of robbers and be destroyed.
basic.originalLanguages: Judah has turned God's house into a beit peritsim, a den of robbers, not where crimes happen but where criminals flee for false safety after committing them elsewhere. God's foundational command was always to shama, hear and obey, one Hebrew word doing both jobs, showing that true worship was never separable from a listening, responsive life. The horrors at gehinnom, the Valley of Hinnom, later gave its name to Gehenna, Jesus' own word for final judgment.
basic.typology: The temple sermon's warning that God's own house can be destroyed for hollow religion anticipates Jesus' cleansing of the temple, where He quotes this exact den of robbers language, Mark 11:17, and it anticipates the temple's actual destruction in AD 70. The Valley of Hinnom's horrors become, by Jesus' day, the very name and image He uses for final judgment, Gehenna.
basic.christ: Jesus deliberately quotes Jeremiah 7:11 word for word when He cleanses the temple, has this house not become a den of robbers, Mark 11:17, standing in the line of this very sermon and applying it directly to the temple of His own generation. Jesus also repeatedly warns of Gehenna, the very valley named in this passage, showing that the judgment Jeremiah announced here for hollow religion becomes, in Christ's teaching, the abiding warning about final judgment for every generation.
basic.god: God is patient, having sent prophets again and again before this final warning, yet He will not be manipulated by correct religious language detached from a changed life; His own Name dwelling in a place never obligates Him to protect that place regardless of the wickedness inside it. His justice is severe enough to destroy His own house, yet He states plainly that even the horror of child sacrifice at Topheth was never something He commanded or even conceived.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 11:15-17 has Jesus quote Jeremiah 7:11 directly when cleansing the temple. 1 Samuel 4:1-11 records the actual, historical loss of the ark and the fall of Shiloh referenced as precedent. Matthew 5:22 and 10:28 have Jesus use Gehenna, drawn from this very valley, as His term for final judgment. 1 Samuel 15:22 states the same priority, to obey is better than sacrifice, that this sermon insists on.
basic.oneStory: This is the exposure of hollow religion at its most dangerous, trust in a building, a chant, or a ritual as a substitute for a changed life. Jesus stands in direct continuity with this warning, quoting it as He cleanses the temple of His own day and turning the very valley named here into His enduring image of final judgment, calling every generation to the obedience Jeremiah's audience refused.

context.literary: The sermon is carefully structured around the repeated indictment that words and rituals, this is the temple of the LORD, our sacrifices, cannot substitute for a changed life, moving from a direct confrontation at the temple gate (7:1-11) through historical precedent (7:12-15), specific idolatrous practices (7:16-20), theological principle (7:21-28), and a graphic vision of coming judgment (7:29-8:3).
context.historical: Likely delivered early in Jehoiakim's reign, this sermon is closely paralleled in Jeremiah 26, which records the near-fatal reaction it provoked among priests and prophets who wanted Jeremiah killed for it, showing how deeply this challenge to temple-based security threatened entrenched religious and political interests.
context.canonical: This sermon's central principle, obedience over ritual, echoes 1 Samuel 15:22 and Isaiah 1:11-17, and its warning about the temple's destruction anticipates both the actual Babylonian destruction of Solomon's temple and, centuries later, Jesus' warning about the second temple's coming destruction, Matthew 24:1-2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shatter Judah's false confidence that God's presence in the temple guaranteed unconditional national safety regardless of how they lived, using the historical precedent of Shiloh's destruction to show that God's own dwelling place is never exempt from judgment when the people's lives contradict their worship.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This sermon addresses a specific historical presumption about the Jerusalem temple; it is not a general prescription against religious buildings or corporate worship as such. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that no religious location, ritual, or correct theological slogan substitutes for a life of justice and covenant loyalty before God.

debates.question: Does verse 22 mean God never actually wanted sacrifices, or is it emphasizing that obedience was always primary?

views.label: A rhetorical overstatement emphasizing priority
views.case: Since the Torah clearly does command sacrifices, this verse is best read as Hebraic rhetorical overstatement, meaning obedience mattered far more than sacrifice, not that sacrifice was never commanded at all, a pattern also seen in Hosea 6:6 and 1 Samuel 15:22.

views.label: A historical claim about the sequence of the Sinai covenant
views.case: Some note that the foundational Sinai covenant in Exodus 19-20 begins with the call to obey God's voice before the sacrificial system is elaborated in later chapters, so the verse may be making a genuinely historical point about which command came first and was more foundational.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree on the sermon's main point: ritual sacrifice was always meant to express, not replace, a heart and life that actually listens to and obeys God, exactly what this generation had reversed.

study.originalLanguages: Judah's temple has become a beit peritsim, a den of robbers, not the site of crime but the hideout criminals flee to afterward for false security, the very phrase Jesus later applies to the same building. God's foundational command was always to shama, hear and obey, since biblical Hebrew uses one word for both, showing worship was never meant to function apart from a listening, responsive life. The horrors at gehinnom, the Valley of Hinnom, become the source of Jesus' own term Gehenna for final judgment.

typology.type: The temple called a den of robbers and threatened with Shiloh's fate, Jeremiah 7:11-14
typology.fulfillment: Jesus quotes this exact phrase while cleansing the temple, Mark 11:17, and the temple is later actually destroyed, Matthew 24:1-2.

typology.type: Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom, site of child sacrifice, Jeremiah 7:31-32
typology.fulfillment: Becomes the source of Gehenna, Jesus' recurring image for final judgment, Matthew 5:22 and 10:28.

study.christ: Jesus stands in direct continuity with this sermon when He cleanses the temple, quoting Jeremiah 7:11 word for word, has this house not become a den of robbers, Mark 11:17, applying the same charge to the religious presumption of His own generation. He also repeatedly warns of Gehenna, taking the very name of the valley of atrocity described here and making it His enduring image for final judgment, showing that the warning Jeremiah gave at the temple gate carries forward as Christ's own warning to every generation that substitutes ritual for genuine obedience.
study.god: God is patient, having sent prophet after prophet before this final confrontation, yet He refuses to be bound by correct religious language detached from a transformed life; even His own Name dwelling in a place does not obligate Him to shield it from judgment. His justice is severe enough to destroy His own house as He did at Shiloh, yet He is careful to state that even the worst atrocities committed in His name, child sacrifice at Topheth, were never something He commanded or conceived.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes Jeremiah 7:11 directly while cleansing the temple of His own generation.
Records the historical loss of the ark and fall of Shiloh that this sermon cites as a sobering precedent.
Jesus repeatedly uses Gehenna, drawn from the Valley of Hinnom named here, as His term for final judgment.
States the same principle this sermon insists on, that to obey is better than sacrifice.

study.oneStory: This is the exposure of hollow religion at its most dangerous, trust in sacred buildings, rituals, or slogans as a substitute for a changed life before God. Jesus stands in direct continuity with this very sermon, quoting it as He cleanses the temple and turning the valley named here into His lasting image of final judgment, calling every generation to the obedience Jeremiah's hearers refused.

sermon.title: In the Temple
sermon.why: Begg reads Jeremiah 7:1-11 alongside Mark 11:15-19, showing that Jesus' cleansing of the temple deliberately quotes and enacts this very temple sermon, exactly the connection this study draws between Jeremiah's warning and Christ's own confrontation with hollow religion.

quiz.q: What false confidence does Jeremiah confront the people with at the temple gate?

options.text: That chanting 'this is the temple of the LORD' guarantees safety regardless of how they live.
options.note: Verse 4 names this exact chant as deceptive words the people wrongly trusted.

options.text: That the temple had already been destroyed and could not be rebuilt.
options.note: The temple is still standing at this point; the false confidence is trust in its presence, not concern over its destruction.

options.text: That sacrifices were no longer being offered at all.
options.note: Sacrifices were still being offered, verse 21, but as a substitute for obedience rather than an expression of it.

options.text: That foreign nations had already been defeated permanently.
options.note: The passage is about religious presumption tied to the temple building, not military claims about foreign nations.

quiz.q: What historical precedent does God cite to warn that the temple is not automatically safe?

options.text: Shiloh, the earlier dwelling place of God's Name, which He destroyed for Israel's wickedness.
options.note: Verses 12-14 explicitly cite Shiloh's destruction as the pattern that could repeat at Jerusalem's temple.

options.text: The building of Solomon's original temple.
options.note: The temple's construction is not the precedent cited; its destruction, foreshadowed by Shiloh, is the warning.

options.text: The exodus from Egypt under Moses.
options.note: The exodus is referenced later regarding obedience, verse 22, but Shiloh is the specific precedent for the temple's possible destruction.

options.text: The reign of King David.
options.note: David's reign is not the precedent cited here; Shiloh's destruction under the judges is the specific warning example.

quiz.q: According to verses 22-23, what did God command Israel's fathers most fundamentally at the exodus?

options.text: To obey His voice, so that He would be their God and they His people.
options.note: Verse 23 states this was the foundational command, prior to and more basic than the sacrificial system.

options.text: To build the tabernacle before anything else.
options.note: The tabernacle's construction followed the giving of the law, but the fundamental command cited here is to obey God's voice.

options.text: To offer burnt offerings as the primary requirement.
options.note: The text explicitly says this was not merely a command about sacrifices; obedience was the deeper requirement.

options.text: To conquer Canaan immediately upon leaving Egypt.
options.note: Conquest is not the subject of this verse; the command cited concerns obedience to God's voice.

quiz.q: What horrific practice at Topheth does God say He never commanded?

options.text: Burning their sons and daughters in the fire as child sacrifice.
options.note: Verse 31 states plainly this was never commanded and never even entered God's mind.

options.text: Offering grain and incense at the temple.
options.note: These were legitimate, commanded elements of worship; the horror described is child sacrifice, not grain offerings.

options.text: Fasting during times of national mourning.
options.note: Fasting is not the practice condemned in this passage; child sacrifice at Topheth is.

options.text: Celebrating annual harvest festivals.
options.note: Harvest festivals were legitimately commanded elsewhere; this passage condemns the specific atrocity of child sacrifice.

quiz.q: How does Jesus later connect Himself directly to this sermon?

options.text: He quotes Jeremiah 7:11 word for word while cleansing the temple of His own day.
options.note: Mark 11:17 records Jesus using the exact den of robbers language from this passage during the temple cleansing.

options.text: He has no recorded connection to this sermon anywhere in the Gospels.
options.note: Jesus explicitly quotes this passage in Mark 11:17.

options.text: He rebuilds Topheth as a place of worship.
options.note: Jesus never rebuilds Topheth; He uses the valley's name, Gehenna, as an image of judgment instead.

options.text: He forbids any future prophet from ever criticizing the temple again.
options.note: Jesus does the opposite, continuing and intensifying this very critique of hollow temple-based religion.

questions: Judah trusted a religious chant, this is the temple of the LORD, as a substitute for a changed life. What correct-sounding religious language or routine might you be leaning on instead of actual obedience?

God warned that His own temple, precious and central as it was, could be destroyed like Shiloh if the lives behind the worship did not change. What in your life have you assumed is untouchable simply because it feels sacred or familiar?
God says obedience, hearing and doing, was always more foundational than ritual sacrifice. Where do you substitute religious activity for actually listening to and obeying what God has said?
Jesus later quoted this very sermon while cleansing the temple of His own generation. How does knowing Jesus takes hollow religion this seriously change how you examine your own worship?

God says the atrocities at Topheth never even entered His mind, exposing how far people can drift while still claiming to serve Him. Where might you need an honest, outside voice to show you how far a practice or belief has drifted from what God actually asked?

translationNotes.note: The threefold repetition, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, mimics chant-like liturgical repetition, underscoring that the people treated the phrase almost as an incantation rather than a theological claim requiring a matching life.

The Queen of Heaven likely refers to a Mesopotamian or Canaanite astral deity, possibly Ishtar or Astarte, worshipped by the whole household, showing idolatry had become a normalized family practice rather than an isolated individual sin.

Topheth's name may derive from a word meaning fireplace or hearth, or from a word for shame, deliberately used with contempt for the site of child sacrifice; by the New Testament era this valley's name, Gehenna, had become the standard Jewish term for final judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web search this session.
